definition: To *evoke* is to call a spirit or power *out* — to summon it forth into appearance before the operator, typically into a circle, triangle, or vessel outside the self. It is the mirror of *invoke*, which draws a power *in*: the ceremonial magician evokes a demon to visible station, but invokes a god into their own person. Evocation makes the spirit manifest and external.
history: Latin *evocare* joined *e-*, 'out,' to *vocare*, 'to call' — literally 'to call out.' The sharpest turn is Roman: the ritual of *evocatio* called an enemy city's tutelary gods *out* of its walls to defect before a siege, state-sponsored divine poaching. Ceremonial magic preserved the directional precision — Solomonic handbooks describe aerial spirits evoked and constrained into circles — so that to evoke means to bring a spirit out and make it appear. English later softened it toward 'call forth a feeling or image,' but magic keeps the older, literal summons.

def_sources.label: Peterson, The Lesser Key of Solomon (spirits evoked and constrained)

etymology.origin: Latin evocare 'to call out' (e- + vocare 'to call')
etymology.note: The Roman ritual of evocatio literally called an enemy city's gods OUT to defect before a siege — state-sponsored divine poaching.
etymology.hook: The Roman spell that stole other cities' gods.

flavor_snippets.html:
"These Gruul skin-runes—discern whether their patterns <mark>evoke</mark> streets, leylines, or both. You have four days."
—Niv-Mizzet, to his assistant

mechanics_snippets.html:
When this creature leaves the battlefield, create a 4/4 green Elemental creature token.
<mark>Evoke</mark> {4}{G} (You may cast this spell for its <mark>evoke</mark> cost. If you do, it's sacrificed when it enters.)

Menace
When this creature enters, target opponent reveals their hand. You choose a nonland card from it. That player discards that card.
<mark>Evoke</mark>—Exile a black card from your hand.

When this creature enters, target player discards two cards.
